1: biblos geneseos (biblos geneseos) — book of the origin/genealogy — the same phrase as LXX Genesis 5:1 (haute he biblos geneseos anthropon). Matthew deliberately opens with the creation-formula, presenting Jesus as inaugurating a new genesis. Iesou Christou — Jesus Christ — the name first, immediately followed by both titles: huiou Dauid (son of David, the royal Messianic line) and huiou Abraam (son of Abraham, the covenant promise-bearer). The reverse order (David before Abraham) emphasizes the Messianic dimension; then the founding covenant is named.
18: mnesteutheises (mnesteuo) — betrothed, engaged — the Jewish betrothal (erusin) was a legally binding covenant, not merely a modern engagement; breaking it required a divorce (Matthew 1:19 apolyein = to release/divorce). ek pneumatos hagiou — of/from the Holy Spirit — the preposition ek denotes origin: the conception is sourced from the Holy Spirit, not from human generation. ephanerothe — was revealed, appeared — Matthew uses a passive suggesting divine disclosure; the pregnancy was not hidden but theologically explained by the angel as divine action.
21: Iesous — Jesus, the Greek form of Hebrew Yeshua (Joshua), which means YHWH saves or YHWH is salvation. The angel's explanation (autos gar sosei = for he himself will save) plays on the name's etymology: the one named YHWH-saves will in fact save (sosei). ton laon autou — his people — the possessive is striking; even before the birth, the angel calls Israel his people. apo ton hamartion auton — from their sins — not merely from enemies or oppressors (the expected Davidic deliverance) but from sins; the scope of salvation is transformed.
23: parthenos (parthenos) — virgin — Matthew uses the LXX reading of Isaiah 7:14, which translates Hebrew almah (young woman of marriageable age) with the Greek term specifically denoting virginity. Matthew stakes the fulfillment on this reading. Emmanuel — Hebrew Immanu El = God with us — not merely a descriptive title but a theological claim: the presence of God (the Shekinah) is now embodied in the person of Jesus. Matthew frames the entire Gospel between Immanuel (Matthew 1:23) and the presence-promise of Matthew 28:20.
2: The genealogical formula egennesen (aorist of gennao, to father, beget) runs throughout Matthew 1:2-16, establishing the chain of legal covenant descent. The first entry names Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, then specifies Iouda kai tous adelphous autou — Judah and his brothers — the first appearance of "brothers" signals the wider family unit even as the Messianic line passes through the specific son. The Greek egennesen is the technical genealogical verb, equivalent to the Hebrew holid in the OT toledot formulas.
3: The first of four women in the genealogy: ex autes — from/out of her — the preposition ek with the genitive feminine pronoun (autes) singles out the maternal contribution, departing from the uniform egennesen formula to flag the irregular or notable circumstance. Tamar (Gen 38) bore Perez and Zerah to her father-in-law Judah through a deception she resorted to when he withheld her rightful marriage. Matthew includes her without explanation, leaving the reader to supply the narrative context.
4: The genealogical formula continues without interruption from Matthew 1:2 through Matthew 1:16. These verses establish the covenant lineage through which the Messianic promise (2 Samuel 7:12-16; Ps 89) passes. The names in Matthew 1:4-6 (Ram, Amminadab, Nahshon, Salmon) bridge the wilderness generation to the period of the judges and the establishment of the monarchy — each link in the chain is necessary to the Davidic credential that the genealogy establishes for Jesus.
5: Two more women appear: Rahab (a Gentile prostitute of Jericho, Josh 2; Matthew 6:25) and Ruth (a Moabite widow, Ruth 1-4). Both are Gentiles incorporated into the Israelite community through covenant faithfulness. Rahab: ek tes Raab; Ruth: ek tes Routh. The same ek construction marks each woman. Matthew's inclusion of Gentile women in the Messianic genealogy anticipates the Gospel's movement toward all nations (Matthew 28:19).
6: The fourth notable woman: ek tes tou Ouriou — from the wife of Uriah — Bathsheba is not named; she is identified by her dead husband's name, emphasizing the adultery that was the context of Solomon's conception (2 Sam 11-12). The periphrasis keeps the moral reality visible: Jesus's lineage passes through covenant violation as well as covenant faithfulness. The inclusion of all four women signals that the genealogy does not idealize the Messianic line's history.
7: The post-Solomonic period: Rehoboam (Roboam), Abijah (Abia), Asa. These names carry the weight of the divided kingdom period and Israel's repeated covenant failures. Matthew does not editorialize; the genealogy moves forward through the historical chain, including kings who were largely unfaithful, because the covenant promise to David (2 Samuel 7:16) was unconditional in its ultimate scope. The lineage passes through the kings despite their failures.
8: Verse 8 omits three kings between Joram and Uzziah: Ahaziah, Joash, and Amaziah (cf. 1 Chronicles 3:11-12). Matthew's selective genealogy follows the practice of OT genealogies that sometimes omit names for literary or theological purposes. The omissions may reflect a deliberate 14-14-14 structuring (Matthew 1:17) or judgments about dynasties contaminated by Ahab's house (through Ahaziah's mother Athaliah). The LXX and MT genealogies themselves vary, and Matthew's authority is his apostolic presentation, not genealogical completeness.
9: The genealogical chain continues through the late Judahite monarchy: Uzziah (= Azariah in Kings), Jotham, Ahaz, Hezekiah — a period marked by the growing Assyrian threat that culminated in the northern kingdom's fall (722 BCE). The names carry narrative freight for readers familiar with the history, but Matthew's genealogy abstracts them into the covenantal chain: each king is a necessary link in the Davidic promise moving toward its culmination in Jesus.
10: Hezekiah, Manasseh, Amos/Amon, Josiah — the final pre-exilic generation. Manasseh was the most apostate of Judah's kings (2 Kings 21:1-18); Josiah was the great reformer (2 Kgs 22-23). The contrast within a single genealogical sequence demonstrates that the Messianic line did not depend on consistent covenant faithfulness in its bearers. The promise endures through the worst of Israel's kings as well as the best.
11: epi tes metoikesias Babylonos — at the time of the Babylonian exile (metoikesia = deportation, change of residence by force) — Matthew uses metoikesia (also Matthew 1:12, 17) rather than the harsher aichmalōsia (captivity, imprisonment) perhaps to emphasize the displacement dimension rather than the penal one. The exile is the structural pivot of the genealogy's three sections (Matthew 1:17). Jechoniah (= Jehoiachin) is the king taken to Babylon (2 Kings 24:15).
12: The post-exilic section of the genealogy (meta de ten metoikesian Babylonos = after the Babylonian exile) continues from Jechoniah through Shealtiel and Zerubbabel — the restoration figures (Ezra, Nehemiah, Haggai, Zechariah). Zerubbabel was the governor of the returned community and a Davidic figure around whom royal hopes clustered (Haggai 2:23; Zechariah 4:6-10). The genealogy passes through the restoration generation on its way to the birth of Jesus.
13: The post-Zerubbabel section enters the period of Second Temple Judaism where no biblical genealogies exist: Abiud, Eliakim, Azor are otherwise unknown from canonical sources. Matthew either had access to Davidic family genealogical records or worked from traditional sources. The names are historically plausible (all are common Second Temple Jewish names) but cannot be independently verified. The genealogy is making a legal and theological claim about Jesus's Davidic descent, not a purely archival historical record.
14: The genealogy continues through the otherwise unattested post-exilic figures: Zadok, Achim, Eliud. The covenant chain is maintained through the dark centuries of Persian, Ptolemaic, and Seleucid rule when the Davidic dynasty held no political power. The genealogy preserves the claim that the legal heir to the Davidic throne continued to exist even when no Davidic king ruled — a historically significant claim for a 1st-century Jewish audience familiar with the messianic expectation.
15: The final generation before Joseph: Eleazar, Matthan, Jacob. The name Jacob echoes the patriarch, creating an inclusio with Abraham (Matthew 1:2): the genealogy begins with the founding patriarch and ends with the patriarch's name repeated one generation before the climax. Iakōb de egennesen ton Iōseph — Jacob fathered Joseph — the repetition of the patriarch name immediately before Joseph (the patriarch Joseph's namesake) strengthens the typological resonance: as the patriarch Joseph brought salvation to Israel through suffering, so this Joseph becomes the legal father of the one who saves.
16: Iōseph ton andra Marias — Joseph the husband of Mary — a grammatical pivot: all previous egennesen entries say "A was the father of B"; here the formula shifts to ex hes egennethe Iesous — from whom Jesus was born. The masculine genealogical formula is broken precisely at the point of the virgin birth: Joseph is identified as Mary's husband, not as Jesus's biological father. Ho legomenos Christos — the one called Christ — the appended title marks Jesus as the culmination of the entire 42-generation sequence.
17: Pasai oun hai geneai apo Abraam heos Dauid geneai dekatessares — thus all the generations from Abraham to David are fourteen generations — the triple fourteen pattern (14-14-14: Abraham→David, David→exile, exile→Christ) may reflect gematria: the numerical value of David's name in Hebrew (dalet=4, vav=6, dalet=4 = 14), making the genealogy itself a literary-numerical declaration that Jesus is the Son of David. Matthew's arrangement required selective omission of some kings (Matthew 1:8), a recognized genealogical convention.
19: Iōseph de ho aner autes dikaios on — Joseph her husband, being a righteous man — dikaios (righteous) describes one who observes Torah faithfully. His plan to divorce Mary lathrai (secretly, quietly) means using the private bill of divorce rather than public accusation (Deuteronomy 22:23-24 would have required stoning if adultery were publicly alleged). Apolyein auten — to release/divorce her — the legal vocabulary confirms the betrothal's binding status: apolyein is the same verb for divorce of a married woman (Matthew 5:31-32). Joseph's mercy goes beyond Torah's minimum requirement.
20: kat' onar — in a dream — Matthew uses this phrase five times (Matthew 1:20; Matthew 2:12, 13, 19, 22) for divine communication, corresponding to OT dream-revelation (Gen 37-40; Dan 2, 7). Iōseph huios Dauid — Joseph son of David — the angel's address identifies Joseph's Davidic genealogy as the legal basis for Jesus's Davidic sonship: the legal father (even if not biological) transmits the lineage. Mē phobēthēs paralabein Marian ten gynaika sou — do not fear to take Mary as your wife — the aorist infinitive paralabein (to take alongside, to receive) confirms that the consummation of the marriage had not yet occurred.
22: touto de holon gegonen hina plerothe — all this happened in order that it might be fulfilled — the hina (in order that) with subjunctive indicates divine purpose: the events were arranged for fulfillment, not merely correlated. plerothe (aorist passive subjunctive of pleroo) — filled up, completed. Matthew's fulfillment formula (to rhēthen hypo kyriou dia tou prophētou = what was spoken by the Lord through the prophet) traces the divine word through two agents: the Lord (kyrios) as the primary speaker, the prophet as the instrument. This form of the formula appears 11 times in Matthew.
24: egertheis de ho Iōseph apo tou hypnou epoiesen hos prosetaxen auto ho angelos kyriou — Joseph, having been aroused from sleep, did as the angel of the Lord commanded him. The participle egertheis (having been aroused/raised up) is the same root as the resurrection verb (egeiro), though here merely "woke." The obedience formula (epoiesen hos = he did as...) mirrors OT compliance patterns (Genesis 6:22; Exodus 12:50 — Noah and Israel "did as God commanded") — Joseph as the obedient servant of the divine will, regardless of personal cost or public scrutiny.
25: heos hou eteken huion — until she bore a son — the conjunction heos hou (until) establishes a temporal boundary: the statement describes what obtained during the pregnancy. Greek heos hou does not grammatically imply the resumption of the condition after the boundary event; the text makes no statement about the state of the marriage after the birth. Matthew's concern is entirely with the virgin birth and the legal naming of the child. Kai ekalesen to onoma autou Iesoun — and he called his name Jesus — Joseph's naming of the child is the legal act of paternity that establishes Jesus's Davidic lineage.
